original_abstract: Ten multifloral honey samples produced by *Apis mellifera* L. in Roraima, Brazil, were analyzed to determine their physicochemical properties and antioxidant potential. The study focused on quantifying total phenolic content, flavonoid profiles—specifically flavones/flavonols and flavonones/dihydroflavonols—and color intensity, as well as evaluating free-radical scavenging activity. Total phenolics, measured via the Folin-Ciocalteu method, ranged from 250 to 548 mg gallic acid kg⁻¹. Flavonoid concentrations were assessed using two distinct colorimetric assays: the aluminum chloride method yielded values between 9 and 48.6 mg quercetin kg⁻¹, while the 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine method revealed substantially higher levels of flavonones and dihydroflavonols, ranging from 1805 to 2606 mg pinocembrin kg⁻¹. Antioxidant capacity, determined by the DPPH assay, showed IC₅₀ values between 3.17 and 8.79 mg/mL, indicating high potency relative to literature standards. Correlation analysis revealed significant relationships between darker honey color, higher phenolic and flavone/flavonol concentrations, and stronger antioxidant activity. While flavonones and dihydroflavonols were major constituents, they did not significantly correlate with the other tested parameters. These findings provide the first characterization of honey from this region and underscore its potential as a source of dietary antioxidants.
humanized_abstract: Ten multifloral honey samples produced by *Apis mellifera* L. in the state of Roraima (Brazil) were submitted to physicochemical evaluation and antioxidant activity determination. Thus, the aims of the present study were: (i) to assess the total phenolic content through the Folin-Ciocalteu test and to characterise flavonoids, which were further divided into subclasses including flavones/flavonols and flavonones/dihydroflavonols through the aluminium chloride and 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine colourimetric methods; (ii) to establish the honey color intensity, and (iii) to access their free radical scavenging activity through DPPH test. Total phenolic content, assessed by the Folin-Ciocalteu method, varied between 250 and 548 mg gallic acid kg⁻¹ honey. The analysis of flavonoids content using aluminium chloride, revealed concentrations ranging from 9 up to 48.6 mg quercetin kg⁻¹ honey, whereas using 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine, revealed an important flavonones/dihydroflavonols content ranging from 1805 up to 2606 mg pinocembran kg⁻¹ honey. Honey samples, assessed by DPPH assay, showed antioxidant capacities related to IC₅₀ values ranged from 3.17 up to 8.79 mg/mL, which are extremely low when related to other foods, showing the great antioxidant power of honey. Although flavonones and dihydroflavonols were the most abundant compounds, they did not have a significant correlation with the other analytical parameters evaluated. However, light colour of honey samples did present some correlation with higher levels of total phenolic compounds, flavones/flavonols and consequently greater scavenging activity of the honey samples. Thus, this study gives the first detailed characterisation of Roraima state honey samples that can act as a natural, dietary, source of antioxidant compounds for human consumption.
